coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots self-decorated heteroatom-doped porous carbon with superior electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Kaixin
Wang, Liqiang
Xu, Ying
Fang, Yong
Fang, Yin
Xia, Wei
Liu, You-Nian
coredata.dc:description: Developing advanced carbon materials as a metal-free electrocatalyst to replace noble metal-based one for, but not limited to, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is of great allure but remains challenging. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) self-decorated, N, S codoped porous carbon (NSPC) was fabricated by alkali-assisted protein decomposition and reconstruction. Specifically, in hot alkaline solution, proteins hydrolyzed into protein fragments, and then part of the fragments formed highly crystalline CDs. After freeze-dried and pyrolyzed, the CDs-containing protein fragments were converted into CDs/NSPC composites. The as-prepared CDs/NSPC possesses rich edge, heteroatom-dopant, high porosity, and smooth electron transfer between CDs and NSPC, synergistically contributing to CDs/NSPC such an outstanding ORR catalytic performance that even outperforms Pt/C. Furthermore, CDs/NSPC was evaluated as an air electrode catalyst for Zn-air batteries with excellent stability and high-power density.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots-decorating
Heteroatom dopant
Porous carbon
Oxygen reduction
Zn-air battery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-decorated codoped porous carbon (CDs/NSPC)
Preparation and characterization of CDs-decorated protein fragments
Performance of Zn-air batteries using CDs/NSPC as an air electrode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs/NSPC. (b) UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrum of BSA-KOH-100 (inset presents the pictures of the BSA-KOH-100 solution without (left) and with (right) irradiation by UV lamp). (c) Fluorescent spectrum of BSA-KOH-100 under different excitation wavelength of 310–430 nm. (d) TEM image of BSA-KOH-100 (inset shows the HR-TEM and size distribution of the gained CDs).

(a) SEM and (b) TEM images of CDs/NSPC. (c), (d) TEM image of CDs/NSPC at high magnification (inset in (c) presents the size distribution of CDs). (e) HR-TEM of CDs/NSPC. (f) Element mapping images of C, N, O and S in CDs/NSPC.

(a) XPS spectra of N 1s of CDs/NSPC. (b) Contents of various nitrogen species in the catalysts. (c) XPS spectrum of S 2p for CDs/NSPC. (d) Contents of various sulfur species in catalysts. (e) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (f) the corresponding pore size distribution of the carbon materials. (g) XRD profiles and (h) Raman spectra of the carbon materials. (i) Schematic illustration of CDs/NSPC for ORR catalysis.

(a) RDE polarization curves of the samples and Pt/C. (b) RDE curves of CDs/NSPC under different rotation rates from 400 to 1600 rmp at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 (the inset presents the corresponding K-L plots). (c) Calculated electron transfer number and H2O2 yields over CDs/NSPC, and the inset represents the RRDE polarization curves of CDs/NSPC. (d) The electron transfer number (n) of samples and Pt/C calculated according to the corresponding RRDE polarization curves under the overpotential of 0.75 (V vs. RHE). DFT calculation of ORR activity for different active sites: (e) The free-energy diagram of the eight different models and (f) ORR volcano plot of correlating the overpotential and adsorption of *OOH. (g) The Tafel slopes of the samples and Pt/C. (h) The EIS spectra of the samples. (i) Comparison of ORR catalysis performance over CDs/NSPC and the reported CDs/carbon support composites.

(a) Schematic diagram of the home-made Zn-air battery. (b) Polarization and power density profiles of ZABs using CDs/NSPC, NSPC, NSC and Pt/C as the air electrode catalysts. (c) Long-term discharge curves of Zn-air battery using CDs/NSPC and Pt/C, the inset presents the home-made Zn-air batteries which can light up an LED. (d) Discharge curves of Zn-air battery using CDs/NSPC, NSPC, NSC and Pt/C as the air electrode catalysts at different current densities.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots self-decorated heteroatom-doped porous carbon with superior electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Technology, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Materials and Technology for Energy Conversion, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing advanced carbon materials as a metal-free electrocatalyst to replace noble metal-based one for, but not limited to, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is of great allure but remains challenging. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) self-decorated, N, S codoped porous carbon (NSPC) was fabricated by alkali-assisted protein decomposition and reconstruction. Specifically, in hot alkaline solution, proteins hydrolyzed into protein fragments, and then part of the fragments formed highly crystalline CDs. After freeze-dried and pyrolyzed, the CDs-containing protein fragments were converted into CDs/NSPC composites. The as-prepared CDs/NSPC possesses rich edge, heteroatom-dopant, high porosity, and smooth electron transfer between CDs and NSPC, synergistically contributing to CDs/NSPC such an outstanding ORR catalytic performance that even outperforms Pt/C. Furthermore, CDs/NSPC was evaluated as an air electrode catalyst for Zn-air batteries with excellent stability and high-power density.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials are emerging as metal-free catalysts to substitute Pt/C for oxygen reaction reduction (ORR) [1–5]. Generally, pristine carbon is intrinsically inert towards ORR catalysis. To unlock its ORR catalytic activity, researchers have proposed several strategies, such as heteroatom doping, edge engineering, defect engineering and pore creating. Recently, edge engineering is receiving increasing attention [6–10]. The edge structure benefits the charge delocalization and/or spin-redistribution, which can improve the adsorption and activation of O2 molecules on the surface of catalysts [11]. For instance, Yao and coworkers found that, as the ORR catalytic sites, the edged pentagon in un-doped carbon was even more efficient than pyridinic nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon materials [9]. Meanwhile, edged heteroatom sites are more active than those on the interior basal plane in promoting the ORR process [8,10]. Therefore, one can expect a higher ORR catalytic activity by endowing carbon matrices with more edges.

Owing to the relatively small size, carbon dots (CDs) possess abundant edges. Though CDs themselves exhibit limited activity for ORR due to their poor conductivity, they can be grafted onto conductive carbon matrices to afford edge-rich composite structures with enhanced ORR performance. For example, CDs have been successfully integrated with conductive graphene, carbon nanotube and heteroatom-doped porous carbon matrices [12–17]. The gained composites often have highly exposed and efficient active sites. Therefore, several strategies were proposed for preparing such CDs/carbon composites. Currently, the constructing strategies mainly relied on fusing the as-prepared CDs and carbon supports by high-temperature pyrolysis, which are often chemically engineered and from the same precursors. For example, Guo and coworkers prepared N, S-codoped CDs and graphene, and then integrated them via continuous hydrothermal reaction and pyrolysis to give the final CDs-embedded composites [16]. Alternatively, Wang et al. fabricated GQDs/graphene nanoribbon composite by one-step reduction of two small organic components by sodium, where both GQDs and graphene nanoribbons came from the same precursors [13]. Nevertheless, there is still room in improving the ORR catalysis performance of the gained composites, and correspondingly, it is commendably desired to propose simple and efficient strategies that can afford CDs/carbon composites with superb ORR catalysis activity.

Herein, alkali-driven protein decomposition and reconstruction are proposed as a strategy for preparing CDs self-decorated, N, S-codoped porous carbon matrices (named as CDs/NSPC) with high electrocatalytic activity for ORR (Fig. 1 a). Specifically, bovine serum albumin (BSA) hydrolyzed into small fragments in a hot KOH aqueous solution, and gradually part of those fragments converted into fluorescent CDs. After freeze-dried and pyrolyzed, the CDs-containing protein fragments were converted into CDs/NSPC composites with more edges. Theoretical calculation demonstrates that edges of graphene sheets are more active in ORR catalysis than their interior basal plane. The rich edge, heteroatom dopant, high porosity, and the smooth electron transfer between CDs and NSPC lead to the high catalytic activity of CDs/NSPC for ORR. Furthermore, when utilized as an air electrode catalyst for Zn-air battery, CDs/NSPC shows higher stability and power density than that of Pt/C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-decorated codoped porous carbon (CDs/NSPC)
ce:section.ce:para: BSA was obtained from Sangon Biotech without further purification. Unless noted otherwise, all the other reagents were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and of analytical grade. The procedure for the preparation of CDs/NSPC can be divided into two steps: Firstly, BSA (0.6 g) and KOH aqueous solution (1 mL, 1.6 M) were mixed with 7 mL of deionized water. The solution was heated to 100 °C for 12 h under stirring to afford a CDs-containing solution, labeled as BSA-KOH-100. Then BSA-KOH-100 was freeze-dried and pyrolyzed at 900 °C (heating rate: 5 °C/min) for 2 h to give CDs/NSPC composite.

The optical property of CDs was evaluated by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan), fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan) and circular dichroism spectrum (J-815, JASCO, Japan). The morphology of the carbon materials was investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Nanolab 600i, FEI HRLIOS, USA), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI HRLIOS, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D500, Siemens, Switzerland), Raman micro spectra (HR 800, Jobin Yvon labRam-010, France) and X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250, ThermoFisher, USA) were explored to monitor the crystal structure, defect and surface composition, respectively. The BET surface area and DFT pore size distribution were measured by Physisorption Analyzer (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, USA). All cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were conducted on RST electrochemical station (F5010, RST, China). The rotate disk electrode (RDE) and rotate ring disk electrode (RRDE) electrochemical measurement were tested by a rotating disk electrode (PINE Instrument, VersaSTAT, USA). CDs-decorated protein powders were obtained by firstly putting a CDs-decorated solution into a refrigerator under −80 °C overnight, followed by freeze-drying on a freeze drier. The freeze-drying process was repeated several times to completely remove the water.

All the electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1 M KOH solution using a three-electrode system. The glassy carbon electrode loaded with catalysts was used as a working electrode. Graphite rod and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as a counter electrode and a reference electrode, respectively. Catalyst ink was prepared as follows: 5 mg catalyst was dispersed into 980 μL mixture solution of ethanol and deionized water (V:V, 1:1). Then 20 μL Nafion solution (5%) was added under ultrasonication. The working electrodes were prepared by dropping catalyst ink (5 mg mL−1) onto electrode (e.g., glassy carbon electrode (GCE), the RDE electrode, RRDE electrode) and dried naturally. The loading mass was about 0.21 mg cm−2. The methanol crossover effect was tested by chronoamperometry (CA) method by adding 2 mL of methanol introduced into the electrolyte during the measurement process. The stability was evaluated by CA method (lasting for 80000 s) and CV cycles (10000 cycles). The calculation details on electron transfer number (n), H2O2 yields and conversion of the testing potentials into RHE potentials were shown into Supporting Information.

The homemade Zn-air batteries were made by assembling air cathode, electrolyte and Zn anode. The air cathode owns a sandwich structure and is consisted of catalyst layer (catalyst, carbon black and polytetrafluoroethylene = 5:2:3), current collector (nickel foam) and gas diffusion layer (Midea, Kunming, China). The catalyst layer was prepared as follows: 10 mg catalysts, 4 mg carbon black and 10 mg PTFE (60% solution) were mixed homogeneously, then pressed into a slice with an area of 5 cm2. The final loading amount of catalyst was 2 mg cm−2. After that the catalyst layer, nickel foam (current collector) and gas diffusion layer were pressed in order to form air cathode. 6 M KOH solution and polished zinc plate with 0.5 mm thick were utilized as the electrolyte and anode electrode, respectively. The gas diffusion area of the Zn-air battery is 1 cm−2. The polarization curves are obtained on RST electrochemical station. The discharge stability is tested on a battery test system (LAND-CT2001A, Jinnuo, Wuhan, China) via galvanostatic discharge under a different current density of 2, 5, 10, 20 and 50 mA cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs-decorated protein fragments
ce:section.ce:para: BSA and KOH aqueous solution were mixed and heated to 100 °C, then kept at 100 °C for 12 h. After that, a yellow solution was obtained, denoted as BSA-KOH-100. Meanwhile, two control samples were prepared under the same condition but without the addition of KOH (denoted as BSA-100) or without heating process (named as BSA-KOH-25). UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to test the optical property of the samples. As shown in Fig. 1b, BSA-KOH-100 gives a blue-green fluorescence upon exposure to UV lamp, demonstrating that CDs formed. Their emission varies from 410 to 500 nm when the excitation wavelength ranges from 310 to 430 nm (Fig. 1c). The unique excitation-dependent emission is a typical feature for CDs [18]. The morphologies of the CDs were investigated by TEM. The CDs exhibit a relatively homogeneous size distribution with an average size of 12.2 nm (Fig. 1d). HR-TEM images show that CDs are crystalline. The crystal lattice with a spacing of 0.23 nm could be determined, which is ascribed to the (100) facet of graphene structure (inset in Fig. 1d). In contrast, BSA-100 and BSA-KOH-25 solution give neglected emission, suggesting that few CDs formed and their formation was pH- and temperature-dependent (Fig. S1). Circular dichroism spectra and gel electrophoresis analysis were exploited to monitor the structural changes of BSA before and after converted into CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the BSA solution exhibits strong absorption peaks at 208 and 222 nm, corresponding to its α-helix structure. The absorption peaks gradually disappear upon incubating with a hot alkaline solution, suggesting the destruction of the secondary structure. Furthermore, as depicted by the gel electrophoresis, BSA decomposed into small molecular fragments (Fig. S3). Overall, the formation mechanism of CDs can be clarified as follows. Protein molecules decompose into protein fragments (e.g., peptides or amino acids), then partial fragments form thermally stable CDs. During its formation, alkali can promote the hydrolysis of protein as well as the subsequent ‘hydrothermal carbonization.’ Thus, in the presence of alkali, CDs could form under relatively mild conditions, different from the previously reported methods where CDs formed under relatively high temperature and pressure [19]. In addition, alkali can etch the generated CDs, preventing them from aggregating [20–23]. Due to the relatively mild condition for the formation of CDs, there were a large number of protein fragments not being converted into CDs but surrounding the generated CDs, which was deduced from the FT-IR spectra. Strong absorption within the range of 1100–1500, 1650 and 3100–3500 cm−1 is observed for both BSA and BSA-derived CDs, belonging to function group I (–C–C–, –C–N– and –CC–), group II (–CO), and group III (–OH and –NH2), respectively (Fig. S4) [24]. The CDs-decorated protein fragments afford a precursor for making CDs-decorated carbon matrices. Firstly, CDs in the precursor are highly compatible with the remaining protein fragments. Therefore they can be pyrolyzed into graphitic carbon matrices without compromising the CDs. Besides, alkali can etch the carbon matrices, which not only gives birth to the porous structure but also prevents the CDs from aggregating, jointly promoting the exposure of potential active sites [20,25]. Additionally, CDs are more likely to chemically bond to carbon matrices during the high-temperature carbonization, which favors the electron transfer between the CDs and carbon matrices.

CDs/NSPC composite was obtained after freeze-drying and carbonizing of the CDs-decorated protein solution. NSPC and NSC were obtained from BSA-KOH-25 and BSA-100, respectively. The morphology of the materials was studied by SEM (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S5) and TEM (Fig. 2b and c and Fig. S6). CDs/NSPC displays a loose and porous structure. Besides, the carbon matrices were decorated with CDs with an average size of 4.40 nm (inset in Fig. 2c and d). However, only neglected CDs are observed for both NSPC and NSC (Fig. S6), suggesting that the formation of CDs-decorated carbon matrices is highly related to the generation of CDs in the precursors. Compared with NSPC and CDs/NSPC, NSC possesses a compact structure without apparent pores (Fig. S5). HR-TEM image of CDs/NSPC reveals that the CDs are highly crystalline. The lattice spacing of 0.23 nm is observed, which belongs to the (100) facet of graphene (Fig. 2e). Overall, all results confirm the formation of CDs/NSPC, which agrees well with the previous reports [16]. Proteins are rich in heteroatoms, such as N and S, which allows them to act as precursors for fabricating heteroatom doped carbon materials. For example, BSA has been widely studied as a precursor for preparation of heteroatom self-doped carbon materials, such as carbon dots, porous carbon materials which have a great potential for application in energy, catalysis and biomedicine [21,22,26–29]. As depicted in EDX mapping images, C, N, O and S elements are homogeneously distributed through the carbon matrices (Fig. 2f).

XPS measurement was carried out to investigate the composition of CDs/NSPC. XPS survey spectra demonstrate that there exist C, N, O and S elements within CDs/NSPC, NSPC and NSC (Fig. S7). Compared to BSA-KOH-100, CDs/NSPC, NSPC and NSC have a lower content of N and S. This is because some heteroatoms were converted into volatile gaseous species (e.g., NOx and SOx) during the high-temperature pyrolysis (Table S1), which is particularly the case for CDs/NSPC and NSPC. Their precursors contain alkali which can react with carbon matrices, leading to the loss of the heteroatoms chemically bonded with carbon matrices. Fig. 3 a and Fig. S8 present the N 1s spectra of CDs/NSPC, NSPC, NSC and BSA-KOH-100. They reveal that N 1s can be fitted into four peaks at around 398.4, 399.9, 401.0 and 402.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and oxidized N, respectively [30–32]. In contrast, CDs-containing protein fragments only have one peak at 399.5 eV, belonging to the –CO–NH– of protein fragments [33]. The content of different N species within CDs/NSPC, NSPC and NSC are shown in Fig. 3b. Clearly, the pyridinic N and graphitic N are the dominant N species for all samples, which are reported more efficient in promoting ORR catalysis [4,34–36]. As illustrated in Fig. 3c and Fig. S9, the XPS spectrum of S 2p can be fitted into three major peaks at 163.7, 164.9 and 168.5 eV. The former two are assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene S (C–S–C), respectively; while the last one is ascribed to oxidized S species (C–SOx) [37,38]. From Fig. 3d, it is found that there exists a large amount of thiophene S in NSPC, NSC and CDs/NSPC, which is more effective than oxidized S species in improving the ORR catalytic activity [39]. Of note, heteroatom doping is beneficial for inducing high-performance active sites, synergistically improving the ORR activity of CDs/NSPC [40,41].

The nitrogen adsorption/desorption tests were exploited to investigate the pore structure of CDs/NSPC. As shown in Fig. 3e, CDs/NSPC and NSPC exhibit remarkably enhanced absorption volumes compared with NSC. Because the precursor of CDs/NSPC and NSPC contain alkali which can etch the carbon to give pore structure [21,42]. The specific surface area was determined to be 1172.54, 1196.50 and 148.85 m2 g−1 for CDs/NSPC, NSPC and NSC, respectively. Both CDs/NSPC and NSPC display a type-I isotherm, featuring that the nitrogen uptake rapidly arrives at the plateau under low relative pressure (P/P
0 < 0.1). This result indicates that there exist plenty of micropores within CDs/NSPC. The pore structure was further characterized by pore size distribution (Fig. 3f). Micropore (<2 nm) plays an overwhelming role in both CDs/NSPC and NSPC. Intriguingly, CDs/NSPC possess small amounts of mesopores with a size of 2–3 nm, partly due to the presence of CDs in the precursor of CDs/NSPC. Overall, two factors can lead to pore structures. On the one hand, under high temperature, protein fragments themselves can be pyrolyzed into volatile species such as CO2, SO2, NOx, and H2O, leaving pore structure with them. While, without high-temperature pyrolysis, CDs-decorated protein samples possess small pore volume (Fig. S10). On the other hand, alkali (KOH) can react with carbon matrices to induce pores, which can lead to structural defects. XRD and Raman measurements were therefore performed to investigate the structural properties of CDs/NSPC, NSPC, and NSC. As depicted in Fig. 3g, for all samples there appear two diffraction peaks at ca. 24 and 44° (2θ), corresponding to the (002) and (100) facets of graphitic carbon [43]. While the peaks for CDs/NSPC and NSPC is more broaden than NSC. Because alkali (in the precursors) can etch the carbon matrices to induce defects, hence to reduce the degree of graphitization. Two peaks at 1350 and 1580 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of the samples represent the D band and G band, respectively (Fig. 3h). The intensity ratio (I D/I G) was used to reflect the degree of defects of the carbon matrices [44–46]. The values of I D/I G follow the order of CDs/NSPC > NSPC > NSC. XRD and Raman results demonstrate that alkali and CDs in precursors are responsible for the formation of the porous structure, which can generate more defects and edges. CDs/NSPC is likely to work as an electrocatalyst for ORR, due to its plenty of edges and porous structure arising from the unique formation process (Fig. 3i).

The electrocatalytic activity of CDs/NSPC was evaluated in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte using a three-electrode system. Firstly, cycle voltammetry (CV) measurement was performed to evaluate the catalytic activity of the samples with commercial Pt/C as a referring catalyst (Fig. S11). All samples exhibit an obvious reduction peak in O2 saturated electrolyte, but not in N2 saturated one. Of note, CDs/NSPC delivers a more positive O2 reduction potential than its companions and Pt/C. On the contrary, CDs are catalytically inert towards ORR. The RDE measurement further confirmed the excellent ORR catalytic performance of CDs/NSPC. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Table S2, CDs/NSPC delivers a distinguished onset potential (E onset = 0.975 V) and half-wave potential (E 1/2 = 0.850 V), which outperforms Pt/C (E onset = 0.970 V, E 1/2 = 0.840 V), NSPC (E onset = 0.930 V, E 1/2 = 0.820 V), NSC (E onset = 0.816 V, E 1/2 = 0.670 V) and most of the recently reported metal-free ORR catalysts (Table S3). The LSV curve for CDs/NSPC exhibits a fluctuation between 0.2 and 0.7 V, because CDs/NSPC has a porous structure and it is difficult for the ORR process to achieve a pure diffusion-limited condition. Meanwhile, double-layer capacitance current could be detected when performing LSV in the N2-saturated KOH solution (Fig. S12). The LSV curves at various rotation speeds from 400 to 1600 rmp (Fig. 4b and Figs. S13–15) as well as the derived K-L slope (inset in Fig. 4b and Figs. S13–15) were recorded. The electron transfer number for CDs/NSPC is close to 4, similar to that of Pt/C. This result suggests that the electron transfer process is highly efficient, and O2 is mainly reduced into OH− via a 4 e– pathway. To further evaluate the ORR efficiency, RRDE measurement was performed, and the results were shown in Fig. 4c and Figs. S13–15. The electron transfer number and H2O2 yields over CDs/NSPC are determined to be 3.7–3.97 and below 15% respectively, within the whole potential range of 0.2–0.8 (V vs RHE) (Fig. 4d). ORR over CDs usually undergoes a 2e– pathway to give H2O2, due to their poor conductivity and aggregation-induced activity loss [47]. By contrast, CDs/NSPC adopts a 4e– process, more efficient in ORR catalysis than carbon dots alone.

CDs can provide plenty of edges, possibly improving the ORR catalysis of CDs/NSPC. DFT calculation was performed to confirm this hypothesis based on a 4 e– mechanism for ORR (see Supporting information) [48]. Eight extensively investigated carbon structures, including un-doped graphene sheet with different edge structures (e.g., zigzag edge, pentagon edge, and pristine graphene sheet, labeled as E-ZC, E-PC and P–C, respectively) and heteroatom-doped graphene sheet with various doping configurations (e.g., pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and thiophenic sulfur at edge site, and graphitic N at the interior basal plane of pristine graphene sheet, labeled as E-PyN, E-PrN, E-GN, E-ThS and P-GN, respectively) were considered. To identify the possible active sites for each model, different configurations were taken into consideration and were described in Fig. S16. The changes of free energy for each elementary step and the corresponding overpotential for each site were calculated (Table S4). The free energy diagram for all proposed carbon structure is shown in Fig. 4e. The rate-determining step for all involved models is *OOH adsorption except for E-PC, which is *OH adsorption (*represent adsorbed site). Clearly, the pristine graphene sheet is rather inert towards ORR catalysis, represented by a much more positive ΔG 1 value of 1.04 eV for the *OOH absorption. The high ΔG value means the conversion of O2 to *OOH cannot take place spontaneously. By contrast, when endowed with either edge or heteroatom (at both edge and graphene basal plane), the structure exhibits an enhanced ORR catalytic activity with a reduced ΔG 1 value. The theoretical calculation results agree well with the previous reports that the pristine graphene sheet exhibits limited ORR catalysis ability [49]. Furthermore, a typical ORR volcano plot was portrayed by correlating the overpotential and adsorption energy of *OOH. As shown in Fig. 4f, the interior basal plane of the pristine graphene sheet (P–C) displays a large overpotential of 2.27 V. While zigzag or pentagon edge suspended graphene sheet possesses a much lower overpotential of 0.63 and 0.48 V, respectively. Besides, graphitic nitrogen at edge sites (E-GN) displays a lower overpotential (0.78 V) than that at the interior basal plane of the pristine graphene sheet (P-GN) (1.60 V). It is also the case for E-PrN, E-PyN and E-ThS, they exhibit lower overpotentials than P-GN. Of note, the overpotential of edged pentagon carbon (E-PC) is lower than that of all the proposed models except E-PyN, confirming the key role of edge structure in ORR catalysis [9].

Tafel slopes and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were also recorded. As shown in Fig. 4g, CDs/NSPC displays a smaller Tafel slope (77.4 mV dec−1) than that of NSPC and NSC, and even smaller than Pt/C (87.9 mV dec−1), revealing the outstanding ORR kinetic of CDs/NSPC [50]. EIS results reveal that the resistance of CDs/NSPC and NSPC is smaller than that of NSC (Fig. 4h). The difference is because NSPC and CDs/NSPC possess porous structure, which can promote the mass transfer between the electrode surface and electrolyte [51,52]. Besides, the electrochemical surface area of CDs/NSPC is 11.86 mF cm−2, which is larger than that of NSPC (11.57 mF cm−2) and NSC (2.27 mF cm−2), possibly due to the porous structure and CDs decoration of CDs/NSPC composites (Fig. S17). In addition, within the low-frequency range, Tafel slops for CDs/NSPC is larger than that for NSPC, revealing CDs do not restrain the electron and mass transfer within CDs/NSPC, which is crucial for ORR catalysis [53]. Overall, the superb ORR performance of the as-prepared CDs/NSPC is attributed to the following reasons. Firstly, CDs/NSPC possesses rich edges that can work as active sites for ORR. Secondly, the unique porous structure of CDs/NSPC can not only expose more active sites but also promote the mass transfer, improving the ORR catalytic performance. Additionally, the unique formation mechanism of CDs/NSPC allows the effective interaction between CDs and NSPC. It can promote the charge transfer between CDs and NSPC, which is beneficial for the ORR catalysis process. Consequently, the electrocatalytic activity of CDs/NSPC is high and even better than that of the previously reported CDs/carbon support composites (Fig. 4i and Table S5). Generally, compared with Pt-based ORR catalysts, carbon-based ones exhibit high stability and can resist the crossover effect of small molecules like menthol. Thus, chronoamperometric response and CV cycles of CDs/NSPC and Pt/C was performed. As shown in Fig. S18a and Fig. S19, the current remains up to 96.2% after 80000 s, and no obvious decrease in oxygen reduction peak was observed after continuous 10000 CV cycles for CDs/NSPC. By contrast, the current drops to 85.5% for Pt/C, and meanwhile, an obvious reduction potential peak loss was observed. Moreover, CDs/NSPC exhibits an outstanding tolerance ability for methanol crossover effect. The negligible current signaling response was found for CDs/NSPC upon adding methanol into the electrolyte. Differently, a strong response current was observed for Pt/C arising from methanol oxidation (Fig. S18b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of Zn-air batteries using CDs/NSPC as an air electrode
ce:section.ce:para: A home-made Zn-air battery was assembled to evaluate the potentials of CDs/NSPC for practical utilization, where zinc plate works as the anode, carbon materials on foam nickel as the air cathode and 6 M KOH as the electrolyte, respectively (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b represents the discharge polarization and power density curves of the ZABs using different carbon materials as an air electrode. The CDs/NSPC-based air electrode exhibits a slower potential drop than its companions and commercial Pt/C within a broad range of current density. The maximum power density for CDs/NSPC is calculated to be 110.2 mW cm−2, larger than that of the recently reported metal-free catalysts and Pt/C (Tables S2 and 6). The excellent performance of CDs/NSPC-based ZABs mainly comes from its high ORR catalytic activity and unique porous structure, which allows the rapid mass and electron transfer. The stability of the homemade Zn-air batteries was assessed by a galvanostatic discharge under a current density of 20 mA cm−2 (Fig. 5c). No noticeable potential drop was observed for CDs/NSPC, while a gradual potential drop was found for Pt/C especially at the later period of the discharge process. As a potential application, the assembled ZABs were demonstrated able to light up a light-emitting diode (LED) (inset in Fig. 5c). Notably, the specific capacity of the CDs/NSPC was calculated to be 699.2 mAh g−1, slightly higher than that of Pt/C (681.9 mAh g−1). Moreover, the discharge platform at various current densities was recorded for different carbon materials. CDs/NSPC performs much better than its companions and even Pt/C, and the potential delivers by CDs/NSPC is 1.39, 1.35, 1.31, 1.27 and 1.15 V at a current density of 2, 5, 10, 20 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively. Specifically, the potential goes back to 1.35 V when the current density declined to 2 from 50 mA cm−2, further suggesting the high stability of CDs/NSPC (Fig. 5d). Consequently, CDs/NSPC with superb activity and high stability could be exploited as a catalyst for the air electrode of primary ZABs.

Carbon dots self-decorated N, S codoped porous carbon was constructed via alkali-driven protein decomposition and recombination assisted by hydrothermal processing and high-temperature pyrolysis. Proteins molecules were hydrolyzed into protein fragments. Part of these protein fragments can transfer into thermally stable CDs, and after further pyrolyzed, CDs/NSPC composites were gained with rich edge and high porosity. DFT calculation reveals that edge structure is crucial in ORR catalysis, it exhibits lower overpotential than the interior basal plane of graphene sheet. Meanwhile, heteroatoms doped at edge sites are more effective than those on the interior basal plane. The combination of the heteroatom dopant, porous structure and the rapid electron transfer between CDs and NSPC contributes to the superb ORR performance of CDs/NSPC. Specifically, CDs/NSPC exhibits a more positive onset potential of 5 mV and a half-wave potential of 10 mV, and better durability and tolerance to methanol crossover effect than commercial Pt/C. Moreover, CDs/NSPC shows great promise to work as an air electrode for Zn-air batteries with high power density and durability. This study paves a new avenue towards constructing carbon structure-based electrocatalysts for ORR and beyond, with superb performance.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Kaixin Liang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization. Liqiang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ying Xu: Data curation, Methodology. Yong Fang: Data curation, Software. Yin Fang: Data curation. Wei Xia: Data curation, Software. You-Nian Liu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21878342); the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M643001), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2019jj50758), the Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project, China (No. 2019TP1001), and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Central South University (No. 205440).
